rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE GREGSON 1996 TRUST, DATED JUNE 28, 1996
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatories (Added To Calendar For Entry Of Tentative Ruling Purposes Only)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 2 of 2 for purposes of entry of Tentative Ruling.) In most cases, a defendant or respondent takes key allegations in the complaint or petition, and asks the plaintiff or petitioner to state all facts in support of the contention, identify all witnesses with knowledge of those facts, and identify all documents which support the contention. Respondent here has taken virtually every allegation in the petition and asked Petitioner to state all facts, identify all witnesses, and identify all documents which support the allegation. This includes matters well within Respondent's knowledge, including further requests to identify Respondent's and his siblings' addresses and telephone numbers. (See, Separate Statement of Issues, pp. 30-31.) Bringing such matters before the court leads the Court to believe that the moving party's attempts at informal resolution of the discovery disputes was neither reasonable nor in good faith. As framed by the pleadings, this appears to be a simple case with two issues-whether Respondent Trustee has been acting in contravention of the Trust's terms for failing to comply with the explicit one-fourth (1/4) equal division of the Trust Estate among each of the Beneficiaries, and whether Respondent Trustee has breached his fiduciary duties in the administration of the Trust. In all, the facts in this case are no more complicated than a routine trust action. To the extent Respondent believes that Petitioner's responses are not satisfactory, Respondent is free to take Petitioner's deposition, or to leverage any perceived defects during an evidentiary hearing, if one is held. Counsel for Petitioner Anne-Marie O'Halloran shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. (End of Tentative Ruling.)

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE GREGSON 1996 TRUST, DATED JUNE 28, 1996
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion And Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Response To Form Interrogatories Set One And Request For Sanctions Against Her And Hour Counsel In The Amount Of $2,239.20
rulings.Rulings: This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody: (Part 1 of 2 for purposes of entry of Tentative Ruling.) Trustee John M. Gregson's Motion to Compel Further Responses to Special Interrogatories (set one) is DENIED. The granting or denial of a motion to compel rests within the court's sound discretion. The factors to be considered include the relationship of the information sought to the issues framed in the pleadings, and the likelihood that disclosure will be of practical benefit to the party seeking discovery. (Columbia Broadcasting System, Inc. v. Super. Ct. for Los Angeles County (1968) 263 Cal.App.2d 12,19.) As a general rule, a party may only propound 35 special interrogatories as a matter of right. (See Code Civ. Proc., SEC 2030.030(a)(1).) Under section 2030.040(a), subject to the right of the responding party to seek a protective order, a party who attaches a supporting declaration as described in Section 2030.050 may propound a greater number of special interrogatories, if the greater number is warranted because of: (1) the complexity or the quantity of the existing and potential issues in the particular case; (2) the financial burden on a party entailed in conducting the discovery by oral deposition; or (3) the expedience of using this method of discovery to provide to the responding party the opportunity to conduct an inquiry, investigation, or search of files or records to supply the information sought. Here, Respondent propounded 289 Special Interrogatories (in addition to 72 Requests for Admission, 24 Requests for Documents and Form Interrogatories.) Respondent moves to compel further responses to 111 of those special interrogatories. The law requires that each answer in a response to an interrogatory must be as complete and straightforward as the information reasonably available to the responding party permits. (Code Civ. Proc., SEC 2030.220(a).) If an interrogatory cannot be answered completely, it must be answered to the extent possible. (Id. at SEC 2030.220(b).) Evasive answers do not comply with the Discovery Act. (Id. at SEC 2023.010(f); see also, Liberty Mut. Fire Ins. Co. v. LcL Adm'rs, Inc. (2008) 163 Cal.App.4th 1093, 1104.) Here, the Court finds that Petitioner has complied with her discovery obligations and made a good faith attempt to respond to each of Respondent's 111 special interrogatories to the extent that she could with the information she had available to her at the time of responding. (Code Civ. Proc., SEC 2030.220.) The Court certainly finds this to be the case with the first 35 interrogatories. While Respondent may not like the answers, or believe that they could have been answered differently, the answers appear to be code-compliant and the Court declines to compel additional responses with respect to those interrogatories. As a secondary matter, though the Court is ordinarily reluctant to interfere with a party's conduct of the discovery it believes it needs to properly prepare for trial, the Court is struck by the excessive number of specially prepared interrogatories in this case considering the issues and context of the dispute. While the Court appreciates the propriety of legal contention interrogatories which, when used properly, can assist a defendant in truly understanding the basis for and evidence underlying the legal contentions made in a complaint, Respondent has taken contention interrogatories to an absurd extent. (Tentative Ruling continues in Part 2 of 2)

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE GREGSON 1996 TRUST, DATED JUNE 28, 1996
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Trustee John M Gregson To Compel Further Responses From Anne Marie Ohalloran To Trustees Special Interrogatories And Request For Sanctions Against Her And Hour Counsel In The Amount Of $15,096.60
rulings.Rulings: This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody: Trustee John M. Gregson's Motion to Compel Further Responses to Form Interrogatories (set one) is GRANTED. Petitioner is ordered to provide separate responses to Form Interrogatory No. 17.1. for each Request for Admission, Nos. 3, 4, 5, 8, 9, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 19, 20, 21, 22, 23, 24, and 25. Each response shall include all the information called for in Form Interrogatory No. 17.1 (a)-(d). The response is ordered to be served within ten (10) days of entry of this order. The request for sanctions is denied. (See, Karlsson v. Ford Motor Co. (2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 1202, 1217 ["Discovery sanctions must be tailored in order to remedy the offending party's discovery abuse, should not give the aggrieved party more than what it is entitled to, and should not be used to punish the offending party."].) In addition, the parties are ordered to meet and confer regarding the motion to compel requests for admission set for hearing on October 11, 2023, and inform the Court whether it can be withdrawn in light of the tentative ruling above. Counsel for trustee Mr. John M. Gregson shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.
